lesson: Unit 03 Studio — Capstone Build (trig measurement + transformation design)
banner: Week 35 · Unit 03 Capstone — Measure the Real World, Then Transform It
essential_question: Can you find a height you can't reach, and move a shape you can prove?
objective: Take a real-world height or distance measurement using an angle of elevation/depression and right-triangle trig, then design and carry out a transformation (translation, reflection, or rotation) of a figure on the coordinate plane.

content.heading: Trig in the yard, transformations on paper
bullets: Part A: measure something tall or far away that you can't reach directly, using an angle and right-triangle trig.
Part B: design a transformation of a figure on the coordinate plane and defend whether it's rigid (preserves length & angle) or not.
Both parts get written up and proven — numbers alone aren't enough.

agenda.title: Plan — Pick Your Target & Your Figure

content.heading: Gather + plan
content.note: Choose a real object (a tree, a flagpole, a roofline, a distance across the yard) to measure by angle. Separately, choose a polygon on graph paper or a coordinate app to translate, reflect, or rotate.

content.heading: Make both parts real
bullets: Measure the angle of elevation with a protractor or inclinometer app, and the baseline distance.
Compute the height or distance using the correct trig ratio — show your work.
Plot your original figure and its image after the transformation, labeling every coordinate.

content.preview: Session 3: final mixed review, then MUS Unit Test III on your own.

success_criteria.text: I can measure an angle of elevation/depression and a baseline distance, and use trig to find an inaccessible height or distance
I can perform a translation, reflection, or rotation of a figure on the coordinate plane and record before/after coordinates
I can verify whether my transformation preserved side lengths and angles, and explain why

questions.prompt: Record your angle of elevation and baseline distance to the object you measured.
questions.answer: Any consistent measured angle (degrees) plus a baseline distance with units.
questions.explain: Both numbers feed directly into the trig ratio you'll use to find the height or distance.

questions.prompt: Using tan(angle) = height/baseline (plus your eye height, if applicable), compute the height or distance of your object. Show your work.
questions.answer: height = baseline · tan(angle), plus eye height if measuring from eye level; must show substitution and units.
questions.explain: Tangent connects the angle of elevation to the ratio of opposite (height) over adjacent (baseline).

questions.prompt: List the coordinates of your original figure's vertices and its image after your chosen transformation.
questions.answer: Any consistent set of before/after coordinate pairs matching the transformation rule used, e.g. (x,y) → (x,−y) for a reflection over the x-axis.
questions.explain: Each transformation has a coordinate rule — translations add/subtract, reflections flip a sign, rotations swap and/or negate coordinates.

questions.prompt: Reflect: did your transformation preserve every side length and angle? How do you know — what did you check?
